source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
title: Monitor account owner for frequent, unexpected, or unauthorized logins

input_row.source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
input_row.title: Monitor account owner for frequent, unexpected, or unauthorized logins
input_row.description: Monitor login activity of the account owner to prevent unauthorized usage of the privileged account.
input_row.rationale: The owner of an IBM Cloud account by default has administrative privileges across the account. To prevent unauthorized or unintended usage, login credentials should never be shared and users in an organization should be granted least privilege to complete their required tasks. To manage the account, administrators are granted permissions to manage services and Cloud resources. Therefore, access by the account owner to the account to run tasks should be minimum. By controlling the number of times that the account owner logs in to the account, you can: • Monitor owner's access to the IBM Cloud account • Identify abnormal behavior • Enforce that administrators and operators of the services in your Cloud account are the ones managing the Cloud resources with their controlled and limited permissions. When a user log in to the account, a security login event in generated. The event reports who has logged in successfully in the account. You can use these security events to monitor the number of times that the account owner has logged in to the account, and generate an alert if the number exceeds a threshold that you set in a period of time that you specify. Monitoring login activity of the account owner can help prevent unauthorized or unintended actions taken in the account.
input_row.audit: Before you can monitor and manage IAM event data with IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA, you must provision an instance of the service in IBM Cloud in the Frankfurt (eu-de) region. To provision an IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA instance in a Public Cloud region, consider the following information: • You must select the service plan that is associated with the instance, and the plan that determines the retention period for your events. • You can choose from 7, 14, or 30-day retention periods. Alternatively, IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA offers a Lite plan that you can use to view your events as they pass through the system. You can view events by using event tailing. You can also design filters to prepare for upgrading to a longer retention period plan. This plan has a 0-day retention period. Complete the following steps to launch the Activity Tracker with LogDNA web UI: 1. Log in to your IBM Cloud account. After you log in with your user ID and password, the IBM Cloud dashboard opens. 2. Click the Menu icon > Observability . 3. Select Activity Tracker . The list of IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA instances is displayed. 4. Select the instance in the region where you want to view events. Then, click View LogDNA . The IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA web UI opens and shows the Everything view. Through this view, you can see the events in your account for the region that you have selected. To ensure the owner of the account has not logged in more than # times the past 24 hours, complete the following steps: First, you need to identify the email of the account owner. 1. In the Cloud UI, go to Manage > Access (IAM) , then select Users . 2. Identify the user that has the tag owner . 3. Select the account owner. Then, click Details . 4. Copy the email address of the account owner. Second, launch the Activity Tracker instance in Frankfurt. This is the instance where login security events are collected in the account. In the Views section, look for a view that monitors the account owner login attempts. The search query should be set to: (action login) AND initiator.name:<email address>, where is the account owner's email address. Then, check that the view has configured an alert and 1 or more notification channels.
input_row.remediation: Complete the following steps to monitor how many times an account owner logs in to the account: First, you need to identify the email of the account owner. 1. In the Cloud UI, go to Manage > Access (IAM) , then select Users . 2. Identify the user that has the tag owner . 3. Select the account owner. Then, click Details . 4. Copy the email address of the account owner. Launch the Activity Tracker instance in Frankfurt. This is the instance where login security events are collected in the account. In the Views section, select the Everything view. Then, enter the following query in the search bar: (action login) AND initiator.name:<email address>. Replace with the account owner's email address. The view now reports all the login actions that are reported for the account owner. Next, you can define an alert on the view to get a notification when N number of events are received within a 24 hour period. The value of N depends on how you operate your cloud. You can start with a default value of 25 and increase or decrease depending on the tasks that the account administrator can perform in the account. Default Value: no default value. References: 1. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA?topic=Activity-Tracker- with-LogDNA-at_events_iam

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
Given the nature of monitoring login activities using IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA and the detailed steps provided in the audit and remediation sections, the control can be fully verified via IBM Cloud APIs and CLI tools. The process involves querying security events and configuring alerts, both of which are well-supported by IBM Cloud's services and tools.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_activity_tracker_account_owner_login_monitoring_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **IBM Cloud API/CLI:** Use IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA API to access login events. You can leverage the IBM Cloud CLI to access and configure the Activity Tracker.
- **Steps:**
  1. Use the IBM Cloud CLI to log in to the IBM Cloud account and navigate to the Activity Tracker service.
  2. Configure the region for the Activity Tracker instance (preferably Frankfurt as suggested) using the CLI or the web interface to ensure you're accessing the correct data.
  3. Query the security events for actions related to login attempts using the initiator.email filter matching the account owner's email. For example, using a search query like `(action login) AND initiator.name:<email address>`.
  4. Ensure alerts are configured for triggering notifications when login breach threshold is exceeded. This involves checking the alert settings related to the views and notifications.
- **Configuration Properties:**
  - Login attempts by the account owner.
  - Number of login events within a specified 24-hour period.
  - Alerts set for when the login attempts exceed this threshold.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - **Pass:** Alerts are configured properly and notify stakeholders when the account owner logs in more than the set threshold.
  - **Fail:** Either missing alerts for account owner logins or incorrect configuration of threshold values.
- **References/Documentation:** Use the documentation available at [IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA?topic=Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA-at_events_iam) to ensure proper setup and monitoring practices.

This automation ensures that login activities of the account owner are properly monitored through Activity Tracker's auditing capabilities, making it an enforceable security posture check.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. Monitoring login activities can be effectively automated using IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA, which supports querying and alerting capabilities via IBM Cloud APIs and CLI.
- IBM Cloud APIs and CLI provide the necessary functionality to access and configure the Activity Tracker, making the automated approach feasible.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are largely accurate. The use of IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA API and IBM Cloud CLI for accessing and configuring login event monitoring is valid.
- The steps outlined for configuring the region and querying security events are appropriate, but they could be more detailed regarding specific CLI commands.
- The program name `ibm_activity_tracker_account_owner_login_monitoring_enabled` is somewhat descriptive but could be improved for clarity.
- The pass/fail criteria are reasonable, focusing on the presence and correctness of alert configurations.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Detail Specific CLI Commands:** Include specific IBM Cloud CLI commands for logging in, setting the region, and querying events. For example, using `ibmcloud login`, `ibmcloud target -r <region>`, and specific LogDNA API queries.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** Consider renaming the program to `ibm_activity_tracker_login_monitoring_account_owner` to better reflect the specific focus on account owner login monitoring.
- **Threshold Configuration Details:** Provide more explicit guidance on how to configure alert thresholds and what constitutes a breach.
- **Alternative Methods:** Explore using IBM Cloud Functions or IBM Cloud Event Streams for more sophisticated alerting and processing of login events.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of IBM Cloud services and tools is appropriate for the control being audited.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Enhance the assessment by including specific IBM Cloud CLI commands and more detailed instructions for configuring alerts and thresholds.
- Consider renaming the program for clarity and specificity.
- Ensure that the documentation references are up-to-date and provide comprehensive guidance for users implementing this control.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA provides API and CLI access to login events, including filtering by account owner. The audit and remediation steps can be fully automated by querying login events for the account owner and verifying alert configurations. IBM Cloud APIs and CLI support all necessary operations, making manual inspection unnecessary.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
ibm_activity_tracker_login_monitoring_account_owner

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- IBM Cloud Resource/Service: IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA
- API/CLI Method:  
  - Use IBM Cloud CLI (`ibmcloud login`, `ibmcloud target -r eu-de`)  
  - Query Activity Tracker events via LogDNA API or CLI (`ibmcloud activity-tracker events --search 'action:login AND initiator.email:<account_owner_email>'`)
  - Check alert configurations for login frequency thresholds
- Properties to Check:  
  - Presence of login events for the account owner (initiator.email)
  - Frequency of logins over a defined period (e.g., daily/weekly)
  - Existence and correctness of alert rules for excessive/frequent logins by the account owner
- Pass Criteria:  
  - Activity Tracker instance is provisioned in the required region (Frankfurt/eu-de)
  - Login events for the account owner are being captured
  - Alerts are configured to notify on frequent/unexpected/unauthorized logins by the account owner
- Fail Criteria:  
  - No Activity Tracker instance in the required region
  - No login events for the account owner are being captured
  - No alerting or incorrect alert configuration for account owner login activity

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on the automated approach. The only minor difference was the program name; the more descriptive and focused name (`ibm_activity_tracker_login_monitoring_account_owner`) is selected for clarity and alignment with IBM Cloud naming conventions.

**SOURCE:** CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
